My Projects

title: Muireann Ryle
text:
I am currently pursuing a MSc in Business Analytics at Dublin City University where I am developing my understanding of data analytics and business practice through challenge based learning projects. The course includes modules such as Advanced Analytics Techniques, Business strategy, and an Applied Deep Learning Project.
After graduating with a First Class Honours in BSc in Psychology and Mathematics, I decided to continue my learning journey and broaden my knowledge in an area I am particularly interested in, data analytics. Using tools such as Python, SQL, and PowerBI, I have been able to develop research projects on different business challenges and cases.
My Applied Deep Learning Project is a group project focused on developing a classifier for feminine slurs in Portuguese tweets towards footballers.

title: Identifying Fraud
text: This project analysed an artificial dataset of 500 customer transactions, of which 246 were fraudulent and 254 were not. The dataset contained 23 variables (11 categorical and 12 numerical), covering customer demographics, financial indicators, and behavioural attributes. I used a combination of Python and Power BI to perform exploratory data analysis (EDA), identify patterns associated with fraud, and build predictive models for fraud amount.

title: Examining Happiness Across Cultures
text: This project examined how happiness is measured across cultures, focusing specifically on African and Western‑European countries. Using the ISP Happiness dataset, the study applied measurement invariance techniques, confirmatory factor analysis (CFA), and effect size metrics (including dMACS, SDI2, UDI2, WSDI, WUDI) to determine whether two widely used happiness scales — the Subjective Happiness Scale (SHS) and Interdependent Happiness Scale (IHS) — function equivalently across cultures. The findings revealed substantial measurement bias, highlighting the need for more culturally appropriate happiness measures.

title: Identifying Fraud
text:
Data Preparation
Before conducting EDA, I prepared the dataset by:

Normalising continuous variables using z-scores

Calculating the interquartile range (IQR)

Identifying outliers across key variables

Creating additional transformed variables for analysis

# Compute z-scores
z_scores = (fraud_df[cols] - fraud_df[cols].mean()) / fraud_df[cols].std()

# Add to original DataFrame
fraud_df_z = fraud_df.copy()
fraud_df_z[[f'{col}_z' for col in cols]] = z_scores

#IQR
Q1 = fraud_df['transaction_amount'].quantile(0.25)
Q3 = fraud_df['transaction_amount'].quantile(0.75)
IQR = Q3 - Q1

# Define bounds
lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

# Flag outliers
outliers_iqr = fraud_df[(fraud_df['transaction_amount'] < lower_bound) | (fraud_df['transaction_amount'] > upper_bound)]
print(outliers_iqr[['transaction_amount', 'is_fraud']])

title: Identifying Fraud
text:
Exploratory Data Analysis
EDA was conducted in both Power BI and Python to explore relationships between variables. One of the strongest patterns emerged between personal income and fraud cases.
The following code was used to create income brackets and compare fraud vs non‑fraud counts
bins = [0, 20000, 40000, 60000, 80000, float('inf')]
labels = ['<€20k', '€20k–€40k', '€40k–€60k', '€60k–€80k', '€80k+']
fraud_df['income_bracket'] = pd.cut(fraud_df['personal_income'], bins=bins, labels=labels)

income_grouped = fraud_df.groupby(['income_bracket', 'is_fraud']).size().unstack(fill_value=0)
income_grouped.columns = ['Non-Fraud', 'Fraud']
income_grouped['Total'] = income_grouped['Non-Fraud'] + income_grouped['Fraud']
income_grouped['Fraud Rate'] = income_grouped['Fraud'] / income_grouped['Total']

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

income_grouped[['Non-Fraud', 'Fraud']].plot(kind='bar', stacked=False, color=['blue', 'pink'])
plt.title('Fraud vs Non-Fraud Transactions by Income Bracket')
plt.xlabel('Income Bracket')
plt.ylabel('Number of Transactions')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

title: Identifying Fraud
text:
predictive Modelling
Two models were created to predict fraud amount, a simple linear regression model, and a multiple linear regression model. The multiple linear regression had only a minor improvement over the simple linear regression which indicated minimal predictive power from the additional variables. Transaction amount was the best predictor of fraud amount.
import statsmodels.api as sm

# Dependent variable (Y): fraud_amount
Y = fraud_df['fraud_amount']

# Independent variable (X): transaction_amount
X = fraud_df['transaction_amount']

# Add constant for intercept
X = sm.add_constant(X)

# Fit regression model
model = sm.OLS(Y, X).fit()

# Print summary
print(model.summary())

# Select multiple predictors
X = fraud_df[['personal_income', 'family_income']]
Y = fraud_df['fraud_amount']

# Add constant for intercept
X = sm.add_constant(X)

# Fit the model
model = sm.OLS(Y, X).fit()

# Display summary
print(model.summary())

title: Identifying Fraud
text:
Reflection & Skills Developed
This project strengthened my ability to explore a data analytics project — from cleaning and preparing raw data to interpreting statistical outputs and communicating insights clearly. I became more confident in identifying which variables meaningfully contribute to predictive models and which do not, and I learned how to justify these decisions using statistical evidence rather than assumptions.
Working with both Python and Power BI helped me combine code‑driven analysis with visual exploration. Python allowed for precise statistical testing and modelling, while Power BI made it easier to spot patterns across demographic and behavioural variables. This dual approach improved my ability to validate insights using multiple methods.

title: Examining Happiness Across Cultures
text: This project examined how happiness is measured across cultures, focusing specifically on African and Western‑European countries. Using the ISP Happiness dataset, the study applied measurement invariance techniques, confirmatory factor analysis (CFA), and effect size metrics (including dMACS, SDI2, UDI2, WSDI, WUDI) to determine whether two widely used happiness scales — the Subjective Happiness Scale (SHS) and Interdependent Happiness Scale (IHS) — function equivalently across cultures. The findings revealed substantial measurement bias, highlighting the need for more culturally appropriate happiness measures.

title: Examining Happiness Across Cultures
text:
Research Aim
To determine whether the SHS and IHS measure happiness equivalently across African and Western‑European cultures, and to quantify the magnitude of measurement bias using modern effect size techniques.

title: Examining Happiness Across Cultures
text:
Dataset
The project used two publicly available datasets from the ISP Happiness study:

4,122 participants from African and Western‑European countries

Ages 16–67 (M = 22.5, SD = 4.97)

Countries included: Austria, Denmark, France, Germany, Greece, Netherlands, Norway, Portugal, Spain, Switzerland, UK, Kenya, Nigeria, Senegal, South Africa

Measures:

SHS (4 items, 7‑point Likert)
IHS (9 items, 5‑point Likert)

Additional country‑level variables (HDI, climate, population growth, etc.)

Sweden was excluded due to excessive missing data.

title: Examining Happiness Across Cultures
text:
Methodology

Data Preparation

Inspected for missing values and inconsistencies

Removed countries with insufficient sample sizes

Filtered dataset to African and Western‑European regions



Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA)
CFA was used to test: - Configural invariance (same factor structure across groups)
- Metric invariance (equal factor loadings across groups)
Fit indices included: - Chi‑Square
- CFI
- TLI
- RMSEA
- SRMR


Measurement Non‑Equivalence (MNE)
To quantify bias, the study used: - dMACS
- SDI2 / UDI2
- WSDI / WUDI
These metrics capture psychometric distance between cultures.

title: Examining Happiness Across Cultures
text:
Analysis & Findings

Happiness Scores Differed Across Countries
African countries (e.g., Kenya, Senegal) showed higher SHS means than many Western‑European countries.
Example from Table 2:
- Kenya SHS Combined Mean = 5.347
- Germany SHS Combined Mean = 4.691


Measurement Models Did Not Hold Equally Across Cultures

Configural invariance was inconsistent

Metric invariance was often violated

SHS and IHS items behaved differently across African vs Western‑European groups



Large Measurement Bias Detected
Effect size metrics showed large psychometric distances between groups, indicating that individuals with the same latent happiness levels may score differently depending on their cultural group.


Implications

SHS and IHS may not be culturally neutral

Cross‑cultural comparisons of happiness may be misleading

There is a need for culturally adapted or newly developed happiness measures

title: Examining Happiness Across Cultures
text:
Discussion
The study reinforces that happiness is not a universal construct and that Western‑developed measures (like SHS) may not capture the lived experiences of African cultures. Cultural factors such as collectivism, language, spirituality, and socio‑economic context influence how happiness is understood and expressed. The results align with prior research showing that SHS performs best in WEIRD countries, while IHS is more stable cross‑culturally. Your work extends this by quantifying how large these differences are.

title: Examining Happiness Across Cultures
text:
Limitations

Secondary dataset (no control over item wording or sampling)

Uneven sample sizes across countries

CFA models sensitive to large N

dMACS interpretation guidelines still developing

title: Examining Happiness Across Cultures
text:
Future Work

Develop culturally grounded happiness measures

Explore qualitative approaches to define happiness in African contexts

Test invariance using alternative models (e.g., IRT, Bayesian CFA)

Expand analysis to additional global regions

title: Examining Happiness Across Cultures
text:
Reflection
This project strengthened my ability to work with complex psychometric models and large cross‑cultural datasets. I gained experience in CFA, measurement invariance testing, and interpreting advanced effect size metrics. It also deepened my understanding of how cultural context shapes psychological constructs, reinforcing the importance of culturally sensitive measurement in global research.
